summary:
The decline of Olmec civilization (c. 400–300 BC) was a gradual process in which the major Olmec centers — San Lorenzo (abandoned c. 900 BC), La Venta (c. 400 BC), and Tres Zapotes (surviving somewhat longer) — lost their population and monumental construction ceased. The causes remain debated: environmental changes including volcanic activity, river course changes draining the wetland agricultural systems, and shifts in regional trade networks have all been proposed, as have warfare and internal social collapse.

The decline of Olmec political and ritual centers did not mean the disappearance of Olmec cultural traditions. Olmec iconography — the were-jaguar, the feathered serpent, the rain deity, the ballgame, the bar-and-dot numeral system — persisted in successor cultures and was transmitted to later Mesoamerican civilizations including the Maya, Zapotec, Teotihuacan, and ultimately the Aztec. Scholars debate the degree to which the Olmec were the 'mother culture' of Mesoamerica (a view more popular in earlier scholarship) versus one of several parallel regional traditions that shared common cultural elements through interaction. Archaeological evidence increasingly suggests that some Olmec centers were deliberately vandalized — colossal heads were buried and some monuments defaced, possibly indicating conquest or deliberate ritual termination.
